subject: Mathematics

unit.unitTitle: Number and the Laws of Arithmetic
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 17! In this unit you will discover the hidden rules that make arithmetic work. You already know how to add, subtract, multiply and divide. Now you will learn *why* certain shortcuts are allowed, and how a handful of powerful laws let you calculate faster, more cleverly, and with far fewer mistakes. These are the same rules that engineers, shopkeepers and computers all rely on every single day.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 5 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use the commutative law to reorder additions and multiplications and make them easier.
Use the associative law to regroup numbers so that friendly pairs come together.
Use the distributive law to break a number apart for quick mental multiplication.
Follow the correct order of operations using BIDMAS.
Explain why each law works, and know when it does and does not apply.
Spot and correct common calculation errors using mathematical reasoning.
Choose the most efficient method for any calculation you meet.

concepts.title: Why We Need Laws of Arithmetic
concepts.explanation: A mathematical law is a rule that is *always* true, no matter which numbers you use. It is not a trick that works by luck; it is a pattern built into how numbers behave. These laws do four useful jobs for you: They let you calculate more quickly by choosing an easier path to the answer.
concepts.example: They help you check whether an answer is sensible.

concepts.title: The Commutative Law
concepts.explanation: The commutative law says that the order does not matter when you add or when you multiply. You can swap the numbers around and the answer stays exactly the same. For addition: 7 + 38 = 38 + 7 = 45, and 25 + 17 = 17 + 25 = 42. For multiplication: 6 × 9 = 9 × 6 = 54, and 25 × 4 = 4 × 25 = 100. In symbols we write a + b = b + a and a × b = b × a, where the letters can stand for any numbers.
concepts.example: Why is this useful? Because one order is often much easier to think about than the other. Take 7 + 38. Starting at 7 and counting on 38 is slow. But by the commutative law we may write it as 38 + 7, and counting on 7…

concepts.title: The Associative Law
concepts.explanation: The associative law says that the grouping does not matter when you add or when you multiply. When there are three or more numbers, you may decide which pair to work out first, and the final answer will be the same. For addition: (8 + 3) + 4 = 8 + (3 + 4). Check the left side: 11 + 4 = 15. Check the right side: 8 + 7 = 15. Both give 15. For multiplication: (5 × 2) × 3 = 5 × (2 × 3). The left side is 10 × 3 = 30; the right side is 5 × 6 = 30. Both give 30. In symbols, (a + b) + c = a + (b + c) and (a × b) × c = a ×…
concepts.example: The real power of the associative law is that it lets you hunt for friendly pairs – numbers that combine into a round, easy total. Consider 25 × 9 × 4. Working left to right gives 25 × 9 = 225, then 225 × 4 = 900, which…

concepts.title: The Distributive Law
concepts.explanation: The distributive law lets you break one number apart to make a multiplication easier. It connects multiplication with addition and subtraction. In words: to multiply by a sum, you may multiply by each part and then add the results. The formula is a × (b + c) = (a × b) + (a × c). For example, 6 × (10 + 3) = (6 × 10) + (6 × 3) = 60 + 18 = 78. Notice that 10 + 3 = 13, so we have really worked out 6 × 13 by splitting the 13 into a friendly 10 and an easy 3.
concepts.example: This is a superb mental-maths tool. Suppose Farah must work out 36 × 8. She splits 36 into 30 + 6, then distributes the 8 across both parts: 36 × 8 = (30 × 8) + (6 × 8) = 240 + 48 = 288. Each little multiplication is…

concepts.title: Order of Operations (BIDMAS)
concepts.explanation: B – Brackets first: work out anything inside ( ) before anything else. I – Indices next: powers such as squares, if there are any.
concepts.example: D and M – Division and Multiplication, worked from left to right.

concepts.title: Choosing the Best Method
concepts.explanation: Now that you know the laws, the real skill is choosing which one to use. Before you calculate, pause and ask yourself four questions: Can I use the commutative law to swap to an easier order?
concepts.example: Can I use the associative law to group a friendly pair together?

methods.title: How to use the distributive law
steps: Split the awkward number into a round number plus or minus a small number.
Multiply each part by the other number.
Add (or subtract) the two results.
Check your answer is close to a quick estimate.

methods.title: How to follow BIDMAS
steps: Do anything inside Brackets first.
Work out any Indices (powers).
Do Division and Multiplication, left to right.
Do Addition and Subtraction, left to right.

methods.title: Can I break a number apart with the distributive law?
methods.example: Work out 47 × 8 using the distributive law.
steps: Estimate first, then calculate, then check.
Check the can i break a number apart with the distributive law? result carefully.
Check the can i break a number apart with the distributive law? result carefully.

methods.title: Distributive law with subtraction
methods.example: Work out 29 × 6 using the distributive law.
steps: Solution: 29 is close to 30, so split it as 30 − 1.
Rounding up to 30 made the first step very easy.

methods.title: Order of operations
steps: Solution: BIDMAS says multiply before you add.
First 6 × 4 = 24.
If you had added first you would have got 44, which is wrong.

methods.title: Brackets change the order
methods.example: Work out (7 + 3) × 6 and compare it with 7 + 3 × 6.
steps: Solution: With brackets, do inside first: 7 + 3 = 10, then 10 × 6 = 60.
Without brackets, multiply first: 3 × 6 = 18, then 7 + 18 = 25.
The brackets completely change the answer, from 25 to 60.

workedExamples.title: Commutative law to reorder
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Counting on 79 from 6 is slow, so swap the order: 6 + 79 = 79 + 6. Now count on 6 from 79: 79 + 6 = 85. The commutative law let us start from the larger number.

workedExamples.title: Associative law to group a friendly pair
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look for a friendly pair. 50 × 2 = 100. Regroup as (50 × 2) × 17 = 100 × 17 = 1,700. Grouping the 50 and 2 first turned a hard sum into an easy one.

workedExamples.title: Distributive law with addition
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 47 × 8 using the distributive law.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Split 47 into 40 + 7. Then 47 × 8 = (40 × 8) + (7 × 8) = 320 + 56 = 376. Remember to multiply *both* parts by 8.

workedExamples.title: Distributive law with subtraction
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 29 × 6 using the distributive law.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 29 is close to 30, so split it as 30 − 1. Then 29 × 6 = (30 × 6) − (1 × 6) = 180 − 6 = 174. Rounding up to 30 made the first step very easy.

workedExamples.title: Order of operations
workedExamples.solution: Solution: BIDMAS says multiply before you add. First 6 × 4 = 24. Then 5 + 24 = 29. If you had added first you would have got 44, which is wrong.

workedExamples.title: Brackets change the order
workedExamples.prompt: Work out (7 + 3) × 6 and compare it with 7 + 3 × 6.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: With brackets, do inside first: 7 + 3 = 10, then 10 × 6 = 60. Without brackets, multiply first: 3 × 6 = 18, then 7 + 18 = 25. The brackets completely change the answer, from 25 to 60.

workedExamples.title: Choose the best method
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Spot the friendly pair 4 × 25 = 100. Reorder and regroup: (4 × 25) × 27 = 100 × 27 = 2,700. Here the commutative and associative laws worked together.

workedExamples.title: Find and fix the error
workedExamples.prompt: Sagal writes: 18 ÷ 6 + 2 = 18 ÷ 8 = 2.25. What went wrong, and what is the correct answer?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Sagal added 6 + 2 first, but BIDMAS says division comes before addition. The division must be done first: 18 ÷ 6 = 3, then 3 + 2 = 5. The correct answer is 5.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Work out, doing the brackets first: a) (8 + 4) × 3 b) 24 ÷ (3 + 1) c) 15 − (6 + 2).

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Split 48 two different ways and use each to work out 48 × 7.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Multiply by 5 the quick way: a) 24 × 5 b) 36 × 5 c) 52 × 5.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 8 × 23 × 125 efficiently. Which pair did you group, and why?

activities.title: Law Detective (Investigation)
steps: Test the commutative law for addition: work out 15 + 27 and 27 + 15. Are they equal?
Test it for multiplication: work out 8 × 12 and 12 × 8. Are they equal?
Now test subtraction: work out 20 − 8 and 8 − 20. Are they equal?
Write a short 'detective report': which operations follow the commutative law, and which do not? Give an example for each.
You have just proved, with your own numbers, why the law works for + and × but not for − and ÷.

activities.title: Friendly-Pair Card Game
activities.materials: small cards with the numbers 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 25, 50, 125 written on them.
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down.
Turn over two cards and multiply them together.
If the two make a round number (10, 100 or 1000), you win a point and keep the pair.
Play until the cards run out. The player with the most friendly pairs wins.
This game trains your eyes to spot the pairs that make the associative law so powerful.

activities.title: BIDMAS Treasure Hunt
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and coloured pencils for highlighting.
steps: Write out these expressions: 5 + 3 × 4; 12 − 2 × 3; (8 + 4) × 3; 24 ÷ (3 + 1) + 7.
For each one, highlight the operation you must do first in one colour.
Solve each expression step by step, writing every stage.
Check with your AI tutor or a family member. Did the brackets change any answers?

activities.title: Distributive Law Rectangles
activities.materials: squared or dotted paper and coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a rectangle 6 squares tall and 13 squares wide. This shows 6 × 13.
Draw a line to split the width into 10 and 3, making two smaller rectangles.
Colour one part and count its squares (6 × 10 = 60); colour the other (6 × 3 = 18).
Add the two parts: 60 + 18 = 78. Check it equals 6 × 13. You have drawn the distributive law!

activities.title: Market Maths (Family Task)
activities.materials: a family member and some real or pretend prices in shillings.
steps: Ask a family member to name a shopping list, for example 4 items at 25 shillings each.
Use a friendly pair or the distributive law to work out the total in your head.
Say your method out loud: 'I grouped the 4 and the 25 to make 100.'
Swap roles and let your family member try a calculation while you check it.

activities.title: Number Trick Show
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a friend to amaze.
steps: Ask your friend to pick any two-digit number in secret.
Tell them: multiply it by 5, add 36, multiply by 20, then tell you the result.
You subtract 720 in your head, and announce their number multiplied by 100.
Explain the trick using the laws: multiplying by 5 then 20 is the same as multiplying by 100.
Understanding *why* the trick works is more impressive than the trick itself – that is real mathematical power.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Commutative Law Order does not matter for adding or multiplying. a + b = b + a and a x b = b x a. It does NOT work for subtraction or division.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Associative Law Grouping does not matter for adding or multiplying. (a + b) + c = a + (b + c) and (a x b) x c = a x (b x c). Use it to group friendly pairs like 4 x 25 = 100.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Distributive Law Break a number apart to multiply it more easily. a x (b + c) = (a x b) + (a x c). a x (b - c) = (a x b) - (a x c).

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Order of Operations (BIDMAS) Brackets, Indices, Division and Multiplication, Addition and Subtraction. Do x and division before + and subtraction. When operations are equally strong, work left to right.

terms: Order does not matter for + and ×
Grouping does not matter for + and ×
Spread a multiplication over a sum or difference

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo port, Liban loads 4 lorries. Each lorry carries 250 crates. Use a pair that makes 1000 to find the total number of crates.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Khadija fills bags of dates for the masjid. She has 8 trays, each with 125 dates. How many dates has she prepared? Use a friendly pair.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan buys 6 goats at the market. Each goat costs 48 shillings... hundred, that is 4800 shillings each. Use the distributive law to work out 48 × 6 first (the price in hundreds).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A classroom at Nuur Academy has 6 rows of desks. Each row has 4 desks, and there are 5 extra desks at the back. Work out 6 × 4 + 5 using BIDMAS to find the total number of desks.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Farah walks (3 + 5) × 20 metres from her home to the well. Do the brackets first to find the distance in metres.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar works out 100 − 6 × 8 to find how many mangoes are left after selling. What is the correct answer, and why is it not 752?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Work out 8 × 23 × 125 efficiently. Which pair did you group, and why?
Marcus says 19 × 4 = 20 × 4 − 1 = 79. Explain his mistake and give the correct answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Marcus subtracted 1 instead of 1 × 4 = 4; correct is 80 − 4 = 76.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Show, using the distributive law, that 12 × (5 + 3) = (12 × 5) + (12 × 3). Work out both sides.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Left side: 12 × 8 = 96. Right side: 60 + 36 = 96. Both equal 96.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Amina says 'You can always swap the numbers around in any calculation.' Is she right? Give an example that proves your point.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: No – swapping only works for + and ×; for example 10 − 3 = 7 but 3 − 10 = −7.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Put brackets into 4 + 6 × 2 to make the answer 20 instead of 16.
Explain the key idea in Choosing the Best Method.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now that you know the laws, the real skill is choosing which one to use. Before you calculate, pause and ask yourself four questions: Can I use the commutative law to swap to an easier order?

options: Order does not matter for + and ×
Grouping does not matter for + and ×
Spread a multiplication over a sum or difference

selfAssessment: I can use the commutative law to reorder additions and multiplications and make them easier.
I can use the associative law to regroup numbers so that friendly pairs come together.
I can use the distributive law to break a number apart for quick mental multiplication.
I can follow the correct order of operations using BIDMAS.
I can explain why each law works, and know when it does and does not apply.
I can spot and correct common calculation errors using mathematical reasoning.
I can choose the most efficient method for any calculation you meet.
